questions.question: In a relational database, an *entity* (like "customer" or "order") is usually represented as…
questions.explanation: Each entity type becomes a table; each individual occurrence is a row, and each fact about it is a column.

questions.question: What is a primary key?
choices: The first column of a table
A column that is indexed for fast lookup
A column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row
The column used most often in WHERE clauses
questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row — it must be unique, non-NULL, and stable. Indexing comes along for free, but it's not the definition.

questions.question: Which of these makes the best primary key for a `customers` table?
questions.explanation: A surrogate key (auto-numbered id) never changes and is never reused. Emails change, names collide — real-world values make risky keys.

questions.question: A key made from a real-world value like an ISBN or email is called a…
questions.explanation: Natural keys come from the data itself; surrogate keys are made-up numbers. Natural keys look attractive but are fragile because reality changes.

questions.question: What is a foreign key?
choices: A key imported from another database
A column that must contain unique values
A backup copy of the primary key
A column whose values must match a key in another table
questions.explanation: A foreign key points at another table's primary key — like `orders.customer_id` referencing `customers.id` — and the database enforces that the target row exists.

questions.question: A customer can place many orders, and every order belongs to exactly one customer. This relationship is…
questions.explanation: Classic one-to-many: one customer, many orders. It's implemented with a foreign key on the many side (`orders.customer_id`).

questions.question: In a one-to-many relationship, which table holds the foreign key?
choices: Neither — it goes in a third table
questions.explanation: The many side points back at its parent: each order row carries the id of its one customer. Putting order ids on the customer row would need a list in a cell.

questions.question: Students take many courses and courses have many students. How do you model this?
choices: A foreign key on `students` pointing at `courses`
A junction table with a foreign key to each side
Duplicate student rows, one per course
questions.explanation: Many-to-many always needs a third table (like `enrollments`) with one row per student-course pair — no single foreign key can express it.

questions.question: What's the ideal primary key for that `enrollments` junction table?
questions.explanation: The pair is what's unique — one row per student-per-course. (An auto-id plus a UNIQUE constraint on the pair also works.)

questions.question: A `phones` column containing `'555-1234, 555-9876'` violates which principle?
choices: First normal form — one value per cell
questions.explanation: 1NF says every cell holds a single atomic value. Multiple phone numbers belong in a separate `phone_numbers` table, one row each.

questions.question: Columns named `item1`, `item2`, `item3` on an `orders` table are a symptom of…
questions.explanation: Repeating groups cap how many items fit and make queries miserable ('search all three columns'). The fix is an `order_items` table with one row per item.

questions.question: The main danger of storing a customer's address on every one of their order rows is…
choices: the table gets too many columns
an update anomaly — changing it in one place but not others leaves contradictions
questions.explanation: Duplicated facts drift apart when updated. Store the address once on `customers` and reference it — that's the entire point of normalization.

questions.question: An `order_items` table has key `(order_id, product_id)` and also stores `product_name`. Which normal form does that break?
choices: 2NF — the name depends on only part of the key
questions.explanation: `product_name` is determined by `product_id` alone — half the composite key. 2NF says move it to the `products` table.

questions.question: An `employees` table stores `dept_id` and also `dept_name`. Which normal form does that break?
choices: 3NF — a non-key column depends on another non-key column
questions.explanation: `dept_name` depends on `dept_id`, not on the employee. 3NF says department facts live in a `departments` table, referenced by id.

questions.question: "Every non-key column must depend on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key" summarizes…
questions.explanation: The three clauses map to 1NF/2NF/3NF: depends on the key (there is one), the whole key (2NF), nothing but the key (3NF).

questions.question: What is denormalization?
choices: Fixing a database that was never normalized
Deliberately duplicating data to speed up reads, after a clean design
Splitting a table into more tables
questions.explanation: Denormalization trades consistency-safety for read performance. It's a measured optimization on top of a normalized design — not an excuse to skip design.

questions.question: An `order_items` table stores `unit_price` even though `products` has a `price`. This is…
choices: correct — the price at time of sale is a different fact from the current price
questions.explanation: Historical price and current price are different facts. If products.price changes next week, old orders must still show what was actually paid.

questions.question: Referential integrity means…
choices: every table has a primary key
every foreign key value points at an existing row in the referenced table
questions.explanation: No orphans: an `orders.customer_id` of 42 is only valid while customer 42 exists. Declared foreign keys let the database enforce this.

questions.question: If deleting a customer should be blocked while they still have orders, you'd rely on…
choices: the foreign key constraint rejecting the delete
questions.explanation: That's foreign key enforcement at work (the default RESTRICT behavior). Alternatives like ON DELETE CASCADE instead delete the orders too — a deliberate design choice.

questions.question: When is a NULL appropriate in a well-designed column?
choices: Never — NULLs are always design errors
When the value is genuinely unknown or not applicable for some rows
Whenever you're not sure what type to use
questions.explanation: NULL legitimately means 'unknown/absent' — like `return_date` on an open loan. But default to NOT NULL, and never allow NULL in a key.

questions.question: Which is the best type strategy for storing money amounts in a serious system?
choices: Floating-point REAL, since prices have decimals
TEXT, so you can include the currency symbol
An exact type — integer cents or DECIMAL — to avoid rounding drift
questions.explanation: Binary floating point can't represent 0.10 exactly; tiny errors accumulate across sums. Exact types (integer cents, DECIMAL/NUMERIC) are the standard answer.

questions.question: A one-to-one relationship between two tables most often suggests…
choices: the two tables could simply be one, unless splitting off optional or sensitive data
an error in the foreign keys
questions.explanation: True 1:1 splits are for optional detail blocks, huge rarely-used columns, or restricted-access data. Otherwise, merge them.

questions.question: An employee table where each employee's `manager_id` points at another employee is an example of…
questions.explanation: The foreign key targets the same table it lives in — queried with a self-join, as you did in the joins unit.

questions.question: Which columns should you index *first* in a typical schema?
choices: Foreign keys and columns you constantly filter or join on
questions.explanation: FKs get joined constantly and aren't auto-indexed in most databases. Indexing everything backfires — each index slows every write.

questions.question: What's the cost of adding an index?
choices: Writes get slower and storage grows
The table must be recreated on every insert
questions.explanation: Every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also maintain each index. Indexes are a read-vs-write trade, which is why you add them deliberately.

questions.question: A `courses` table stores `teacher_name` and `teacher_office`. Teachers teach many courses. What's the 3NF fix?
choices: Add a UNIQUE constraint on teacher_name
Create a `teachers` table and reference it with `teacher_id`
Split courses into one table per teacher
Store the office only on one of each teacher's courses
questions.explanation: Office depends on the teacher, not the course. Extract the teacher entity; courses then carry just a `teacher_id` foreign key.

questions.question: Which design correctly models "a book can have several authors, an author writes several books"?
questions.explanation: Many-to-many again — only a junction table with a row per (book, author) pair captures it without duplication or lists-in-cells.

questions.question: Your report query needs 6 joins and feels slow, so a teammate suggests merging everything into one wide table. The standard advice is…
choices: Do it — fewer joins is always better design
Keep the normalized design; consider indexes, views, or targeted denormalization only after measuring
Delete the foreign keys to speed up joins
questions.explanation: Joins are what relational databases are built to do fast. Measure first; if needed, add indexes or a reporting view — wholesale denormalization reintroduces every update anomaly.

questions.question: Which naming practice makes a schema easiest to work with?
choices: Consistent snake_case with foreign keys named `<entity>_id`
Naming tables after the app screens that use them
Mixing singular and plural table names for variety
questions.explanation: Boring consistency wins: `customer_id` in any table obviously points at `customers.id`, and nobody has to memorize exceptions.

questions.question: You're designing a system for gyms: members join classes, each class has one instructor, instructors teach many classes. Which table set is right?
choices: members, classes, instructors — with `classes.instructor_id` and a `class_members` junction table
questions.explanation: Instructor→classes is one-to-many (FK on classes); members↔classes is many-to-many (junction table). Three entities, four tables total.
